title: The Withered Hand
subtitle: A man with a shrivelled hand stands in the synagogue, and Christ says, "Stretch out your hand."

stillness: The Lord set the man in the midst, that the miracle might be made more plain.
Stand, then, in the midst, and wait for him.
gospel: It came to pass, on another sabbath,
that Jesus entered into their synagogue and taught.
And there was there a man
having a hand withered and drawn in.
keeping guard over him by guile,
whether he would heal on the sabbath,
commanded the sick man to rise
and to stand forth in the midst.
Then he said to him: Stretch out your hand.
And as he stretched it out,
at once it was restored to health.
behold: He entered the synagogue on the sabbath to teach,
for on that day the gathering of the people was greater.
Though the Pharisees lay in wait for him,
he did not fear to come where they were.
And there in the synagogue stood a man
whose hand was withered and drawn in.
The Pharisees were watching him in their malice,
asking whether it is lawful to heal on the sabbath:
that if he said no, they might charge him with cruelty,
and if he said yes, accuse him of breaking the sabbath.
In his deeds they were observers,
in his words reproachers,
in his torments mockers.
He commanded the sick man to rise and stand in the midst,
that the miracle might be more manifest,
and the blindness of the malignant more plainly shown.
He set before them the likeness of the animal:
if a beast falls into a pit, you draw it out on the sabbath;
how much more a man, who is nobler and better,
made to the image of God?
This man, the Nazarenes tell, was a stonemason,
begging help with these words:
"I was a mason, earning my bread with my hands;
I beg you, Lord Jesus, restore me to health,
that I need not beg my food in shame."
Then he said: Stretch out your hand.
And as he stretched it, at once it was made whole.
consider: The hand was withered in the synagogue,
for where the gift of knowledge is greater,
there the transgressor lies under heavier guilt.
This withered hand is the whole human race,
dried up in good works,
because in our first parent the hand was stretched out to the forbidden fruit.
But in the mercy of the Lord it is restored,
through the innocent hands of Christ stretched out upon the cross.
The hand that is withered is commanded to be stretched forth,
for the barren weakness of the soul
is healed by no better remedy than the giving of alms.
His right hand hung useless, for it was numb to almsgiving;
his left was sound, for he looked only to his own gain.
But when the Lord came, the right was healed like the left:
what before he hoarded greedily, now he shares in love.
Learn here the four things the penitent needs:
to rise from sin, by penance;
to stand in grace, by perseverance;
to stand in the midst, by good example;
to stretch out the hand, by good works.
If God were offended in this work of the sabbath,
without doubt the sickness would not have fled;
for kindness cannot follow upon an injury to God.
prayer: I beg you, by the depths of your mercy,
that you would mercifully heal me,
withered and powerless to do good works,
and make me strong and fit for every good work.
Make me stand in the midst of the virtues,
that, at your command and by your working,
I may stretch out my hands to justice and not to iniquity,
may do your good pleasure in all things;
may wholly abstain from offending you.
that above all the riches and delights of this world
I may attend to the word of God,
and to the salvation of myself and of others.

latinJewel.latin: Dico verbo, et sanatur infirmus; et Legem sabbati vestri non transgredior, et sine opere opus virtutis consummo.
latinJewel.english: I speak a word, and the sick man is healed; I do not break your law of the sabbath, and without labour I complete a work of power.
